questions.text: I tend to take on the overnight shift or the early morning slot so others can have the easier schedule.
I notice that even the memory of a strong emotion can produce noticeable physical sensations.
I honor others' contributions and integrate them with minimal changes.
I notice that other people sometimes need to tell me a situation was intense because I didn't register it that way.
I view taking charge as a service to the group.
I rarely pick up on passive-aggressive comments in the moment.
I notice the social signals that indicate someone is ready to end a conversation.
I experience reunion with loved ones as an emotional tidal wave — relief, joy, and tenderness all at once.
I believe that life rewards those who are ready to seize unexpected opportunities.
I see my job as walking the path alongside the group, wherever it leads.
I find that competing against my own previous best is one of the most motivating things I do.
I feel that enjoying the fruits of my own labor is exactly what hard work is for.
I trust myself to stay focused regardless of what's around me.
I feel that structure provides the scaffolding on which creativity can flourish.
I do not naturally detect when someone uses a group's shared values or language to gain credibility.
I prefer to live forward without spending much time documenting or reviewing the past.
I hold the view that taking suffering seriously means being willing to change my behavior, not just my feelings, in response to it.
I routinely rearrange my environment to maximize my own comfort and productivity.
I believe that the capacity to examine suffering clinically — without flinching — is a prerequisite for the most demanding forms of service.
I find that I can absorb criticism and sit with it without my body going into defense mode.
I believe that someone needs to take charge, and it might as well be me.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that gives my ethical principles their substance and their force.
I experience joy with an intensity that sometimes surprises the people around me.
I experience anticipation as a mild interest rather than an overwhelming buzz.
I often catch the brief moment of hesitation before someone agrees to something.
I think that the discipline of emotional detachment during crises is a capacity that can and should be trained.
I verify facts and proofread carefully before sharing information with others.
I believe the world would be better if more people put others ahead of themselves.
I prefer to control the narrative around a project's progress.
I find that my emotional steadiness in the face of others' pain is what allows me to offer help that is both timely and well-considered.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most clearly reveals what I truly care about.
I plan my learning around specific skill gaps I've identified.
I speak in a way that signals the discussion remains open for others to shape.
I rarely sense when a person's choice of words reveals their stance on an issue they have not explicitly addressed.
I would take on a second job to support a family member's dream.
I can tell when a host is performing hospitality versus genuinely enjoying having guests.
